hook: Tuff cave packed with 40,000 anonymous plague skulls and a folk cult that adopted them
facts: Charnel cave in the tuff hillside holding bones cleared from the city, swollen by the 1656 plague and 1830s cholera
Devotees 'adopted' nameless skulls, polishing them and asking favours, their names revealed in dreams
Cardinal Ursi shut the practice down in 1969 as fetishism; the cave reopened to visitors and entry is free
Estimated 40,000 remains stacked in the galleries
legend: Skulls thanked their carers by whispering winning lottery numbers; the 'Captain' skull avenged a young man who mocked it, killing him on his wedding day
w: When undertakers cleared the city's dead into this tuff cave, the plague of 1656 buried the count under 40,000 nameless skulls. Neapolitan women began adopting them, polishing the bone, asking favours, learning the dead's names in dreams. The Church called it fetishism and shut it in 1969. The city of skulls.

hook: Alchemist prince's chapel holding a marble Christ veiled so finely people swear it's cloth
facts: Sanmartino carved the Veiled Christ from a single marble block in 1753; the translucent shroud baffles experts
Commissioned by Raimondo di Sangro, Enlightenment prince, inventor and reputed alchemist
The crypt holds two 'anatomical machines', skeletons wrapped in an intact-looking circulatory system of wax and wire
Located at Via Francesco de Sanctis 19, near San Domenico Maggiore
legend: Locals claimed di Sangro injected his servants with a metallising fluid that turned their veins to stone, producing the anatomical machines
w: Raimondo di Sangro, prince and reputed alchemist, filled his family chapel with marble riddles. The centrepiece, Sanmartino's 1753 Christ, lies under a shroud so sheer that visitors still reach to check it isn't cloth. Downstairs wait two skeletons laced with a perfect wax circulatory web. Genius, or something darker.

hook: Tiny alley church whose crypt keeps a skull that grew ears
facts: Founded in 1327 by jurist Bartolomeo di Capua, adviser to the Angevin court
The crypt holds a 'teschio con le orecchie', a skull preserving temporal bones that resemble mummified ears
Believers thought the eared skull a better listener and prayed to it as a channel to the dead
Wrecked in the 1980 earthquake, restored and reopened by a volunteer association
legend: Women came to whisper to the eared skull, certain it heard their pleas more keenly than the other nameless dead and carried prayers straight to purgatory
w: Down a librai alley sits a church barely wider than its door, founded in 1327 and almost lost to the 1980 quake. Its draw waits in the crypt: a human skull that somehow kept two ear-shaped bones. Believers reckoned it listened better than the rest, and queued to whisper. The skull with ears.

n: Santa Maria delle Anime del Purgatorio ad Arco
hook: Baroque church railed with bronze skulls, run by a brotherhood that buried the city's paupers
facts: Consecrated 1638, home to the Congrega di Purgatorio ad Arco, who buried the poor with no church space
Bronze skulls and crossed bones line the railings and the facade outside on Via dei Tribunali
The hypogeum below hosts the cult of the 'anime pezzentelle', the begging souls of the abandoned dead
Devotees built a small shrine to 'Lucia', a skull crowned with a bride's veil
legend: Lucia, said to be a young noblewoman dead before her wedding, became patron of brides-to-be, her veiled skull heaped with offerings for marriage and fertility
w: Bronze skulls stud the railings of this 1638 church, polished bright by passing hands. A brotherhood here buried paupers the city had no room for, and below ground tended the 'begging souls'. One skull, crowned with a bride's veil and called Lucia, still collects offerings from women wanting a husband. The church of bones.

hook: Untouched 18th-century apothecary of baroque shelving and majolica jars inside a 1522 hospital
facts: Inside the Ospedale di Santa Maria del Popolo degli Incurabili, founded 1522 by Maria Lorenza Longo
Designed around 1729; Vaccaro shaped the rooms, Salandra and Massa painted the majolica drug jars
Walnut-root counter and gilded shelves still hold ceramic vases, some crusted with old resins and powders
Reopened in November 2024 after five years closed, alongside the Museo delle Arti Sanitarie
w: Behind the old hospital for the incurable, a 1729 apothecary survives untouched: walnut counters, elliptical domed ceiling, gilded shelves of hand-painted majolica jars. A few still hold the dried resins and powders of forgotten cures. Closed for years, it reopened in 2024. Where alchemy shaded into medicine.

hook: Southern Italy's largest Christian catacombs, frescoed tunnels under the Sanità hill
facts: Two levels carved from tuff under Capodimonte, the lower dating to the 3rd-4th century
Named for Saint Gennaro after his remains were moved here in the 5th century, drawing pilgrims
Roughly 2,000 burial recesses and 500 sarcophagi, with early Christian frescoes and mosaics
Entered beside the Basilica Madre del Buon Consiglio; run by a youth co-op that revived the Sanità
legend: The arrival of Gennaro's body turned the caves into a place of pilgrimage, the faithful seeking burial close to the martyr's bones to share his protection
w: Carved into the tuff above the Sanità, these tunnels grew from one noble tomb into southern Italy's largest Christian catacombs, 2,000 recesses deep. When Gennaro's bones arrived in the 5th century the faithful clamoured to be buried near the martyr. Now a local youth co-op guides you past the frescoes. Underworld of the saints.

hook: Enlightenment burial grid of 366 numbered pits, one opened for each day of the year
facts: Built 1762 by Florentine architect Ferdinando Fuga to bury the city's anonymous poor
A walled square hides 360-plus brick pits, seven metres deep, each marked by a numbered stone slab
Each pit matched a calendar day; only that day's grave was opened, then sealed for a year
Cited as the rare surviving example of an Enlightenment 'rational' cemetery, now semi-derelict
w: Fuga's 1762 design turned death into arithmetic: a walled yard floored with 360-odd brick pits, each numbered for one day of the year. Only that day's grave was unsealed, fed the city's nameless poor, then closed for twelve months. Reason applied to the unburied. A calendar carved in stone slabs.

hook: Royal escape tunnel turned WWII shelter, full of rusting cars and motorbikes 30 metres down
facts: Commissioned 1853 by Ferdinand II of Bourbon as a secret escape route from the palace to the sea
Cut across an older 17th-century aqueduct and cisterns, never finished for its royal purpose
Sheltered thousands during WWII bombing; residents left graffiti, beds and belongings underground
Police later dumped seized and impounded vehicles here, now a frozen fleet of vintage cars and Vespas
w: Ferdinand II ordered a secret tunnel from his palace to the barracks by the sea, terrified of his own people. The royal bolthole was never finished. Wartime turned it into a bomb shelter, then police filled it with impounded cars and Vespas. Thirty metres down sits a rusting fleet nobody came back for. The king's unused exit.

hook: Working charity since 1601 with a Caravaggio painted for its altar still hanging in place
facts: Founded August 1601 by seven young nobles who tended the sick at the Incurabili hospital
Octagonal church on Via dei Tribunali consecrated 1606, the charity still operating today
Caravaggio's 'Seven Works of Mercy' (1607) hangs over the high altar where it was commissioned, for 400 ducats
One canvas crams all seven acts of mercy into a single crowded Neapolitan street scene
w: Seven young nobles started a charity here in 1601, meeting Fridays to nurse the incurable. They asked Caravaggio for an altarpiece, and he crammed all seven works of mercy into one teeming Naples street, for 400 ducats. The painting still hangs exactly where he hung it. Few masterpieces never left home.

hook: Claimant to the world's first pizzeria, baking on Vesuvius lava stone since 1738
facts: Began 1738 as a stall supplying street hawkers with cheap marinara, opened as a sit-down restaurant in 1830
Often called the world's oldest pizzeria, at Via Port'Alba 18 by the Dante book-stall arch
Ovens lined with lava rock carried down from Mount Vesuvius
Fed the city's poor 'pizza a otto', eaten now and paid for a week later
w: It started in 1738 as a stall feeding street hawkers, who sold cheap marinara folded in their hands, then settled into these rooms in 1830. Many call it the first pizzeria anywhere. The ovens still burn over lava hauled from Vesuvius, and the poor once ate now and paid a week later. Pizza before pizzerias existed.

hook: University anatomy collection of cyclops skulls, malformed infants and Vesuvius-roasted bones
facts: Anatomy collection of the university, opened late in the 18th century at Via Luciano Armanni 5
Cases hold cyclops crania, deformed foetuses and a catalogue of bodily pathologies
Among the specimens, skulls said to come from victims of Vesuvius eruptions
Includes wax anatomical models alongside real preserved and skeletonised remains
w: Opened in the late 1700s for students of the body, this university cabinet has the queasy honesty of its age: a cyclops skull, jars of malformed infants, a long shelf of pathologies. Some crania reportedly belonged to people caught by Vesuvius. Wax models sit beside the real thing. Not for the squeamish.

hook: Catacombs where the rich were buried as frescoes growing out of their own skulls
facts: Beneath the Basilica Santa Maria della Sanità, named for a 5th-century North African bishop
In the 1600s Dominican friars seated real skulls in the wall and painted a body below each one
The frescoes showed the dead's trade or rank, the skull standing in for the head
Reserved for the wealthy and banned in 1637 over hygiene; reached via the basilica
legend: Friars held the head the seat of thought and the soul's true home, so they preserved skulls and let bodies dissolve in the charnel, the painted figures completing each person
w: Under the Sanità basilica, 17th-century friars devised a grim portrait gallery. They walled a dead nobleman's real skull into the tuff, then frescoed his body beneath it, dressed for his trade. The head, they reasoned, held the soul; the rest could rot in the charnel. Banned in 1637 as a health risk. Paintings that wear bone.
